tasks.title: Workspace Exploration
tasks.scenario: Start by identifying the current working directory and listing all files in the active workspace. This helps you orient yourself inside the sandbox environment.
tasks.solution: The 'pwd' command prints the current absolute path (which should be /tmp), and 'ls -la' lists all files including hidden ones (like . and ..) in a long listing format.

tasks.title: Inspect Running Processes
tasks.scenario: Display all active running processes inside the sandbox terminal. Verify that ttyd and the shell (bash) are actively running in your session.
tasks.solution: Running 'ps aux' outputs the process table. You will see '/sbin/tini -- ttyd -W bash' running as PID 1, along with the 'ttyd' daemon and 'bash' interactive shell running under your user ID.

tasks.title: Verify gVisor Kernel Isolation
tasks.scenario: Verify that your container is successfully running inside a gVisor user-space kernel instead of the host kernel. Check the kernel release details.
tasks.solution: The output of 'uname -a' should contain 'gvisor'. This indicates that system calls are virtualized by gVisor's sentry kernel rather than passing directly to the host OS.

questions.text: In a restricted environment where cloud-based AI cannot be used, this local tool would be a highly effective alternative for complex command options.
Based on how well the tool retrieved options for standard commands during the test, I am confident it would successfully help me navigate a niche or internally developed CLI tool.
Compared to the alternatives in a restricted environment (e.g., reading internal wikis, checking man pages, or asking a colleague), using this tool would significantly speed up my workflow.
If my daily job required interacting with undocumented or proprietary command-line applications, I would actively want this local tool installed on my machine.
Considering the scenario above, what do you see as the biggest advantage or potential drawback of relying on this specific tool for internal applications?
